title: Eisenstein series and the cubic moment for PGL(2)
speaker: Paul Nelson

proof_stages.label: Motohashi formula as target cubic moment
proof_stages.summary: The talk identifies Motohashi's fourth-moment formula for the Riemann zeta function as the motivating identity, emphasizes the unexpected cubic moment of PGL(2) central values on its spectral side, and states the program of making the heuristic proof rigorous, weighted, and applicable.

proof_stages.label: Michel-Venkatesh double expansion heuristic
proof_stages.summary: Using Eisenstein series, Hecke and Rankin-Selberg integral representations, Mellin-Plancherel on the diagonal, and spectral expansion on the modular quotient, the same regularized-looking integral of E_0^2 is expanded in two ways to explain the fourth zeta moment on one side and the cubic moment on the other.

proof_stages.label: Regularization of divergent Eisenstein integrals
proof_stages.summary: The divergent diagonal integrals and non-L2 spectral expansions are made rigorous by regularized integration, deformation of the Eisenstein parameters, subtraction of auxiliary Eisenstein series, and bookkeeping of the resulting degenerate terms.

proof_stages.label: PGL(2) group-theoretic weighted identity
proof_stages.summary: The modular-surface formulation is replaced by PGL(2,R)/PGL(2,Z), Eisenstein series are attached to vectors in induced representations, and unfolding the same argument yields Theorem 1: a cubic moment identity with explicit local weight factors h_f and transformed fourth-moment weights \tilde h_f.

proof_stages.label: Local surjectivity for arbitrary weights
proof_stages.summary: Theorem 2 asserts that every sufficiently nice local spectral weight, defined via pre-Kuznetsov test functions on the group, is realized as h_f for some tensor of induced-representation vectors, with an explicit transform \tilde h_f; the proof reduces to producing enough compactly supported Whittaker-type functions and using Kirillov-model manipulations.

proof_stages.label: Ramified local weight and conductor estimates
proof_stages.summary: A nonarchimedean test function adapted to a ramified quadratic character is analyzed to show positivity, a lower bound on the desired twisted Bernstein component, and a controlled transformed weight supported only up to the expected conductor ranges.

proof_stages.label: Global subconvexity and consequences
proof_stages.summary: Combining the global Motohashi formula with the arbitrary-weight construction and local conductor estimates gives a number-field version of the Conrey-Iwaniec cubic-moment subconvexity bound for quadratic twists, together with standard arithmetic and geometric corollaries.

examples.name: Bump weight localizing the zeta function
examples.note: A compact bump test function near a chosen spectral parameter illustrates how Motohashi-type formulae can be used to bound a particular value of the zeta function and why arbitrary weights are useful.
examples.engagement_reason: He only describes hypothetically choosing a bump test function near a point and estimating its transform, without specifying or computing such a weight.

passes.reason: An ordinary smooth test/bump weight used to localize a moment, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Jacobi-theta regularization of the zeta integral
examples.note: The familiar divergent theta-integral representation of the Riemann zeta function is used only to make the required regularization procedure concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He writes the Jacobi-theta zeta integral as a model divergent integral and explains the regularizing steps needed, such as subtracting constant terms and truncating.

passes.reason: The Jacobi-theta zeta integral is a standard regularization model for a divergent integral, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
The Jacobi-theta zeta integral is a standard regularization model illustrating convergence issues, not an extreme or sporadic object and not a refutation.
The Jacobi-theta zeta integral is a standard illustrative regularization model for divergent Eisenstein integrals, not a sporadic or adversarial object.

examples.name: Half-integral-weight Fourier coefficients and ternary quadratic forms
examples.note: These standard consequences of subconvexity illustrate the arithmetic reach of the number-field Conrey-Iwaniec bound, including an improved exponent for representations by ternary quadratic forms.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites half-integral-weight coefficients and ternary quadratic-form representation numbers as corollaries and states old/new bounds, but does not derive them.

passes.reason: Half-integral-weight coefficients and ternary quadratic forms are ordinary application targets for the subconvexity bound, not structural exceptions or counterexamples.

examples.name: Prime geodesic theorem for the Picard threefold
examples.note: The prime-geodesic counting estimate for the arithmetic hyperbolic threefold H^3/PSL2(Z[i]) is cited as a geometric application whose error term improves after inserting the new subconvexity exponent.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the improved subconvex exponent to the Picard threefold prime geodesic estimate and explicitly computes the resulting exponent below 1.6.

passes.reason: The Picard/Bianchi hyperbolic threefold is used as an arithmetic application of the improved bound, not as a sporadic, extremal, or refuting example.
The prime geodesic theorem for the Picard threefold is presented as an application to an arithmetic hyperbolic quotient, not as an obstruction or exceptional case.
